attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Euphron

nodes.label: Cleopatra III Philometor Soteira Dikaiosyne Nikephoros (Kokke)

attributes.Name: Cleopatra III Philometor Soteira Dikaiosyne Nikephoros (Kokke)

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Moschos

attributes.Description: Dioskourides is known from no less than eighteen papyri. His archive provides a unique look into the daily concerns of a phrourarchos, and is dominated by legal matters, including the discipline of the phroura.
attributes.Name: Dioskourides

attributes.Description: He was phrourarchos, a first philos and honored somatophylax in the reign of the Parthian king Arsaces. He seemingly had jurisdiction at least over the village Paliga, which Rostovtzeff and Welles believed to be a fortress at the border.
attributes.Sources: P. Dura 20: “βασιλεύοντος βασιλέ̣ω̣ς βασιλέων Ἀρσάκου εὐεργέτου, δικαίου, ἐπιφανοῦς καὶ φιλέλληνος, ἔτους τξη ὡς ὁ βασιλεὺς βασιλ̣[έων] | ἄγει, ὡς δὲ πρότερο̣ν̣ [υ]λ̣β̣, μ̣ηνὸς Δαισίου ἕκτηι ἐπʼ εἰκάδι, ἐν Παλίγαι κώμηι τῆς περὶ Ἰάρδαν ὑπαρχείας, ἐπὶ Μητολβαίσσα Μην  ̣[  ̣] | τοσδε̣  ̣ου τοῦ Μηναρναίου, φρ̣[ουρά]ρ̣χου καὶ τῶν πρώτων καὶ προτιμωμένων φίλων καὶ τῶν σωματοφυλάκων, καὶ τ̣[ῶν] | ὑπογε̣[γρ]α̣μμένων̣ μ̣α̣[ρτύρ]ων”
attributes.Name: Menarnaios

attributes.Description: Andronicus seems to have served under Antigonus at the siege of Tyre in 315, with command over 3,000 soldiers and was probably the Andronicus of Olynthus, who was assigned by Antigonus as one of Demetrius counselors c. 314. He is next found as phrourarchos in Tyre c. 312, where he offered spirited resistance to Ptolemy, and even leveled personal insults against him. After the mutiny of the stratiotoi in his post he was given to Ptolemy who, instead of seeking revenge for the his previous actions, brought him into royal service.
attributes.Name: Andronicus

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Sources: Dumont 1872, 128 #16: “Φρουράρχ[ου] | Ἐπιγέν[ευς].”; Nicolaou 2005, #746: “[φρου]ρ̣ά[ρ]χ̣ο̣υ | Ἐ̣πιγένευς.”; SEG 26.1135 = SEG 53.1057; SEG 55.1039bis: “ἐπὶ φρουράρχου Ἐπιγένους Ἀριστ[— —].”
attributes.Name: Epigenes

attributes.Description: He is mentioned in a document along with various other officials in 186. Four years later, he served as a witness for an auction of a house, along with many other officers. As the sale took place in Diospolis Magna, he was presumably phrourarchos there.
attributes.Sources: SB 1 4512 A = SB 1 4512 B ll. 11: “...Μεγασθέ]νους φρ̣ο̣υ̣ράρχου...”
attributes.Name: Megasthenes

attributes.Description: He either dedicated, or was the recipient of an honorary inscription.
attributes.Name: Artemidoros

attributes.Description: When Dionysius I seized Motye, he placed Biton of Syracuse as the phrourarchos over the city. Presumably Biton was still at his post the next year when Motye was captured by Carthaginian forces led by Himilcon. As Motye was a Carthaginian colony and military base, no doubt the phrourarchia was imposed onto its normal constitutional framework, although nothing more is known about the administration of the polis under the rule of Dionysius.
attributes.Sources: Diod. Sic. 14.53.5: “μετὰ δὲ ταῦτα φύλακας τῆς πόλεως καταστήσας, Βίτωνα τὸν Συρακόσιον φρούραρχον ἀπέδειξε: τὸ δὲ πλεῖον μέρος ἐκ τῶν Σικελῶν ὑπῆρχεν. καὶ Λεπτίνην μὲν τὸν ναύαρχον μετὰ νεῶν εἴκοσι καὶ ἑκατὸν ἐκέλευσεν παρατηρεῖν τὴν διάβασιν τῶν Καρχηδονίων, συνέταξε δ᾽ αὐτῷ τὴν Αἴγεσταν καὶ τὴν Ἔντελλαν πολιορκεῖν, καθάπερ ἐξ ἀρχῆς πορθεῖν αὐτὰς ἐνεστήσατο.”
attributes.Name: Biton

attributes.Description: Appointed by Mithridates, he committed many “outrages and murders” against the civic population. He later served Mithridates by assisting the king's female relatives to commit suicide on his orders.
attributes.Name: Bacchides [1]

attributes.Description: He was phrourarchos of Apollonia, where he abandoned his post at the approach of Julius Caesar.
attributes.Sources: App. B. Civ. 2.8.54: “... Σταβέριος ὁ φρούραρχος ...”
attributes.Name: Straberius

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Hippocrates

attributes.Description: Josephus relates that he was appointed by Antiochos IV over unspecified areas and was killed by Matthias. However, he was present in Judea later in the revolt, and could not have been killed at its outbreak. Therefore, his status as phrourarchos is suspect.
attributes.Sources: Joseph. BJ 1.36: “καὶ Βακχίδης ὁ πεμφθεὶς ὑπὸ Ἀντιόχου φρούραρχος...”
attributes.Name: Bacchides [2]

attributes.Description: According to the Suda, phrourarchos of Syracuse, who with the assistance of Sosistratus handed over the city to Pyrrhus. He was later killed by Pyrrhus, who falsely alleged that the phrourarchos was engaged in plots against him. He is termed a hegemonikos by Plutarch, whose account matched that in the Suda.
attributes.Sources: Suda, sv. Πύρρος: “ἐσαχθεὶς γὰρ ἐς Συρακούσας ὑπό τε Σωσιστράτου κρατοῦντος τῆς πόλεως τότε καὶ Θοίνωνος τοῦ φρουράρχου...”; Ibid,., s.v. Θοίνωνος: “Θοίνωνος, φρουράρχου Συρακουσίου.”
attributes.Name: Thoinon

attributes.Description: A dedication of a temple under Ptolemy V at Philae mentions him as hegemon and phrourarchos in the polis.
attributes.Sources: Thèbes à Syène 314: “...Κλέων Διογένους Θεσσαλὸς v ἡγεμὼν | καὶ φρούραρχος Φιλῶν...”
attributes.Name: Kleon

attributes.Description: A companion of Alexander, who was left as phrourarchos after the capture Susa.
attributes.Sources: Arr. Anab. 3.16.9: “... καταλιπὼν σατράπην μὲν τῆς Σουσιανῆς Ἀβουλίτην ἄνδρα Πέρσην, φρούραρχον δὲ ἐν τῇ ἄκρᾳ τῶν Σούσων Μάζαρον τῶν ἑταίρων καὶ στρατηγὸν Ἀρχέλαον τὸν Θεοδώρου...”
attributes.Name: Mazarus

attributes.Description:
Originating from Pergamum, Herodes served as a mercenary phrourarchos under Ptolemy VI. He was 

“...the son of Demophon, one of the diadochoi, hegemon of the men, phrourarchos of Syene, gerrophulax (leader of soldiers carrying wicker-work shields), tetagmenos (appointed over) the upper territories, prophet of Chnoubis, and archistolistes (chief temple attendant) of the shrines in Elephantine, Abaton, and Philai...” 

Herodes reappears later in another inscription, dated c. 143-142, revealing his promotion to archisomatophulax and strategos in Syene.
attributes.Sources: OGIS 111 = Thèbes à Syène 302 ll. 7-20: “...ὑπὲρ Βοήθου τοῦ Νικοστράτου | Χρυσαορέως, τοῦ ἀρ̣χισωμ̣ατοφύλακος | καὶ στρατηγοῦ καὶ [κτί]στου τῶν ἐν τῆ[ι] | Τριακοντασχοίνωι πόλεων Φιλομητορίδ[ος] | καὶ Κλεοπάτρας, εὐ[ν]οίας ἕνε[κ]εν | ἧς ἔχων διατελ[εῖ] πρ[ός τε τὸν βασιλέα] | καὶ τὴν βασίλισσαν κ[αὶ τὰ τέκνα α]ὐτῶν, | Ἡρώιδης Δημοφῶντος Π̣ε̣ρ̣[γα]μηνὸς | τῶν διαδό[χω]ν καὶ ἡγεμὼν ἐ[π’ ἀ]νδρῶν | καὶ φρούραρχος Συήνης [καὶ γερρ]οφύλαξ | καὶ ἐπὶ τῶν ἄνω τόπων [τεταγμένος] καὶ | προφήτης τοῦ Χν[ούβεως] κ[αὶ ἀρχ]ιστολιστ[ὴ]ς | τῶν ἐν Ἐλεφαντίνηι [καὶ Ἀβάτωι] καὶ Φίλαις | ἱερῶν...”
attributes.Name: Herodes

attributes.Description: Placed as phrourarchos over Athens after Antipater forced the city to come to terms following the battle of Crannon. He is also mentioned by Plutarch, although he is not listed as a phrourarchos but as hegemon of the Macedonian phrouros. Despite the imposition of the garrison, it did not harm the Athenians due to the influence of Menyllus and his friendship with the Athenian Phocion. Despite the later's rejection of an offer of money from Menyllus, the two remained good friends. After the death of Antipater, Cassander sent Nikanor to replace Menyllus.
attributes.Name: Menyllos

attributes.Description: The phrouroi in Priene issued an honorary decree for him; he served as phrourarchos at lest twice.
attributes.Name: Helikon
attributes.Date: Second half of the third century

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Asklepiodoros

attributes.Description: Appointed by Alexander the Great.
attributes.Sources: Arr. Anab. 3.5.3: “φρουράρχους δὲ τῶν ἑταίρων ἐν Μέμφει μὲν Πανταλέοντα κατέστησε τὸν Πυδναῖον, ἐν Πηλουσίῳ δὲ Πολέμωνα τὸν Μεγακλέους Πελλαῖον...”
attributes.Name: Pantaleon

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Aristion

attributes.Description:
This phrourarchos is only known from Cnidianamphorae stamps.1

1Dumont 1872, 136 #59: “Φρουράρχου | [Διοφ]άντου?”
attributes.Name: [Dioph]antos

attributes.Description: Historian, philosopher, supporter of Dionysius I, and “for a long time” phrourarchos of the akra at Syracuse.
attributes.Sources: FGrH 556 T 5c.5: “ὁ γὰρ δὴ Φίλιστος ἐξ ἀρχῆς τε τῆι τυραννίδι καθισταμένηι προθυμότατον ἑαυτὸν παρέσχε, καὶ τὴν ἄκραν διεφύλαξε φρουραρχῶν ἐπὶ πολὺν χρόνον.”
attributes.Name: Philistos

attributes.Description: Phrourarchos of Vacca and a Roman citizen. He was killed with the entire boule of the town by Metellus after they handed the phroura to Jugurtha and fell back under Roman rule.
attributes.Sources: App. Num. 8.2.3: “Ὅτι Μέτελλος Βαγαίων ἀνῄρει τὴν βουλὴν ὅλην ὡς τὴν φρουρὰν προδόντας Ἰογόρθᾳ, καὶ τὸν φρούραρχον Τουρπίλιον, ἄνδρα Ῥωμαῖον οὐκ ἀνυπόπτως ἑαυτὸν ἐγχειρίσαντα τοῖς πολεμίοις, ἐπαπκέτεινε τῇ βουλῇ.”
attributes.Name: Turpilius

attributes.Description: Phrourarchos here, where the citizens decided to renounce their alliance with the Romans. After luring the population into the theater under the pretext of joining the revolt, he attacked the citizens, and killed a large number of them.
attributes.Name: Pinarius

attributes.Description: Mentioned on a pedimental stele in the akropolis. It seems that another official (name not preserved, patronymic begins with Π) has the title of archon of the stratiotoi, so there is some differentiation in offices.
attributes.Name: [O]nesionos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Menekrates

attributes.Description: Phrourarchos and (restored) hegemon of the akra, who with others set up a statute of Berenike, wife of Ptolemy III. In some restorations he is phrourarchos over Kition only,  while others include Idalion.
attributes.Sources: CIG 2614 = OGIS 1.20 = SEG 20.132 = SEG 31.1348: “[βασίλισσαν Β]ερενίκην τὴν βασιλέως Πτολεμαίο[υ τοῦ Πτολεμαίου] |[ἀδελφὴν καὶ γ]υναῖκα Ποσείδιππος φρούραρχο[ς καὶ ἡγεμὼν τῶν ἐπὶ]|[τῆς ἄκρας(?)] καὶ κατὰ Κίτιον καὶ Βοΐσκος καὶ οἱ <σ>υνηγεμ<ό>ν[ες].”
attributes.Name: Poseidippos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps, with an imprecise knowledge of his full name.
attributes.Name: Asklepiada or Asklapiada

attributes.Description: Attested in a list of soldiers in a dedication at the Asklepieion of Epidauros.
attributes.Name: Timokrates

attributes.Description:
This phrourarchos is only known from Cnidianamphorae stamps.1

1Dumont 1872, 136 # 61: “Φρουράρ[χου] | Κ[νιδίον Διο]γ[ένευς]?”
attributes.Name: [Dio]g[enes]

attributes.Description: This diadochos and phrourarchos on Elephantine island dedicated a monument to the monarchs and gods, on behalf of Ptolemaios, a first philos and strategos, along with another Ptolemaios, Timarchos, and Kallikrates, who were also first philoi.
attributes.Sources: Thèbes à Syène 243: “... Ἀσκληπιάδης |Ἀμμωνίου Μακεδὼν τῶ<ν> διαδόχων καὶ φρούραρχος |Ἐλεφαντίνης.”
attributes.Name: Asklepiades

attributes.Description: Listed by Josephus without further elaboration as phrourarchos of Gaza who was besieged by Alexander the Great. He seems to be the eunuch Batis who is mentioned by Curtius and Arrian. After Gaza fell, Alexander lashed Batis to the back of his chariot and drove around the city.
attributes.Name: Babemesis / Batis

attributes.Description: Strabo states that Artageras / Artagerk (an Armenian phrouria) was caused to revolt by its phrourarchos Ador. Ador is likely a spelling variant of Addon, also known as Addus and Donnes, who wounded Gaius Caesar, eventually causing his death. There is some controversy over Ador's actual position in Armenia, in particular whether he was a former Parthian satrap or a local dynast in his own right. Artageras / Artagerk itself was likely part of Strabo's list of phrourai along the Euphrates.
attributes.Sources: Strabo, 11.14.6; Jones and Sterrett 1966, 326 ap. Crit. 2 & 3; Vell. Pat. 2.102.2: “Armeniam deinde Gaius ingressus prima parte introitus rem prospere gessit; mox in conloquio, cui se temere crediderat, circa Artageram graviter a quodam, nomine Adduo, vulneratus, ex eo ut corpus minus habile, ita animum minus utilem rei publicae habere coepit.”; Flor. 2.32.44: “Quippe Dones, quem rex Artageris praefecerat, simulata proditione adortus virum intentum libello, quem ut thensaurorum rationes continentem ipse porrexerat, stricto repente ferro subiit...”
attributes.Name: Ador

attributes.Description: Possibly appointed by Alexander the Great. He oversaw the safety of a significant deposit of funds in addition to ensuring the security of the fleet and the polis itself. Although nothing more can be said about his time or powers at Tyre, he may have been the same Archelaos who Demetrius left in command of the siege of Babylon in 310, a position with more significant military authority than a phrourarchia.
attributes.Sources: Diod. Sic. 18.37.4: “ὁ δὲ τῆς πόλεως ταύτης φρούραρχος Ἀρχέλαος, Μακεδὼν τὸ γένος,...” ; Heckel 2006, s.v. “Archelaus [3]”.
attributes.Name: Archelaos

attributes.Description: Dedicated an altar (bomos) to Ptolemy, his wife, and various gods on Elephantine island on behalf of Boethos, who was archisomatophulakos and strategos.
attributes.Sources: Thèbes à Syène 242: ll.3-5: “...ὑπὲρ Βοήθου τοῦ Νικοστράτου Χρυσαορέως τοῦ ἀρχισωματοφύλακος καὶ | στρατηγοῦ τὸν βωμὸν Χαιρέας Μέλανος Βοιώτιος φρούραρχος |Ἐλεφαντίνης.”
attributes.Name: Chaireas

attributes.Description: In 280 Decius was placed as phrourarchos over a mercenary force sent by Rome to protect Rhegium from external threats and to look after Roman interests. Decius and his men massacred the male citizens and seized the women, leading Dionysius and Appian to judge that “Decius, instead of a phrourarchos, had become the tyrant of Rhegium.” After losing his eyesight to a vengeful Rhegian doctor, Decius killed himself in a Roman prison before he could be executed.
attributes.Sources: Cass. Dio 9.40.11: “Ὅτι ὁ Δέκιος ὁ φρούραρχος τοὺς Ῥηγίνους...”
attributes.Name: Dekios / Decius

attributes.Description:
This phrourarchos is only known from Cnidianamphorae stamps.1

1Dumont 1872, 137 #62: “Φρουράρ[χου] | [Δαμοκρ]άτε[υς]?”
attributes.Name: [Damokr]ate[us]

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Rhodokleos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Philophronos

attributes.Description: Nikanor has several titles in different sources. He held and garrisoned Munychia, and was also phrourarchos there. He is also referred to as praefectus of Cassander by Nepos. There has been recent arguments that equate him with Nikanor of Statgeria, the nephew of Aristotle, against older views that he was the adopted son of Aristotle. Waldemar Heckel's argument that Nikanor was the son of a relatively undistinguished agent of Antigonus is the most convincing reconstruction of his origin.
attributes.Sources: Polyaenus, Strat. 4.11.2: “...Νικάνορα φρουροῦντα τὴν Μουνυχίαν...”; Plut. Vit. Phoc. 31.1: “ὁ Κάσανδρος καὶ προκαταλαμβάνων τὰ πράγματα πέμπει κατὰ τάχος Νικάνορα τῷ Μενύλλῳ διάδοχον τῆς φρουραρχίας...”
attributes.Name: Nikanor

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Thrasikles

attributes.Description: An otherwise unknown phrourarchos in Naxos, not to be confused with Nikokles, the king of Salamis in Cyprus.
attributes.Name: Nikokles

attributes.Description:
[...]aios son of Ammonios was a member of the diadochoi and phrourarchos of the topos for forty-two years. After earning an unspecified distinction he set up a dedication of thanks to Egyptian Monarchson the island of Philae.1

1Bernand 1989 #322: “[— — — — — —]α̣ι̣ο̣ς Ἀμμωνίου | τῶν διαδόχων φρουραρχ̣[ῶ]ν τὸν τόπον | ἔτη μβʹ καὶ ἀνέγκλητος γε̣γονὼς τοῖς | ἐνταῦθα κατοικοῦσι ὀμοίως δὲ καὶ τοῖς | παρεπιδημοῦσι ξένοις καὶ ἐν ταῖς τῶν | στρατηγῶν παρουσίαις ἐπαίνου τετευχὼς | καὶ ἐπὶ τῆι γενομένηι τοῦ κυρίου βασιλέως | θεοῦ Φιλομήτορος Σωτῆρος τοῦ βʹ (ἔτους) | ἐφόδωι ἐπισημασίας τετευχώς, | εὐχὴν καὶ χαριστήρια.”
attributes.Sources: Thèbes à Syène 322: “[— — — — — —]α̣ι̣ο̣ς Ἀμμωνίου | τῶν διαδόχων φρουραρχ̣[ῶ]ν τὸν τόπον | ἔτη μβʹ καὶ ἀνέγκλητος γε̣γονὼς τοῖς | ἐνταῦθα κατοικοῦσι ὀμοίως δὲ καὶ τοῖς | παρεπιδημοῦσι ξένοις καὶ ἐν ταῖς τῶν | στρατηγῶν παρουσίαις ἐπαίνου τετευχὼς | καὶ ἐπὶ τῆι γενομένηι τοῦ κυρίου βασιλέως | θεοῦ Φιλομήτορος Σωτῆρος τοῦ βʹ (ἔτους) | ἐφόδωι ἐπισημασίας τετευχώς, | εὐχὴν καὶ χαριστήρια.”
attributes.Name: [...]aios

attributes.Description: In his dedication to Zeus Soter and Tyche Protogenos, he describes himself as among the first philoi, a chiliarchos, and a phrourarchos. Later, he placed another dedicatory inscription at Philai c. 139-120 where he still evidently stylizes himself  phroruarchos (heavily restored).
attributes.Sources: IC III.iv.14, ll. 1-7: “Φιλώτας|Γενθίου |Ἐπιδάμνιος |τῶν πρώτων |φίλων καὶ χιλί-|αρχος καὶ φρούραρ-|χος...”; Thèbes à Syène 318: “[․․․․ τοῦ συγγενοῦς κ]αὶ αὐτο-|[κράτορος(?) στρατηγο]ῦ τῆς|[Θηβαίδος(?) Φιλώτα]ς Γενθίου|[— — — — — — — τῶν] πρώτων |[φίλων καὶ φρούραρχο]ς.”
attributes.Name: Philotas

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Philarchida

attributes.Description: Assigned as phrourarchos here by Alexander the Great.
attributes.Sources: Arr. Anab. 3.5.3: “φρουράρχους δὲ τῶν ἑταίρων ἐν Μέμφει μὲν Πανταλέοντα κατέστησε τὸν Πυδναῖον, ἐν Πηλουσίῳ δὲ Πολέμωνα τὸν Μεγακλέους Πελλαῖον...”
attributes.Name: Polemon

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Kleupolis

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Aristratos

attributes.Description: Minucius was a phrourarchos under Caesar before the battle of Dyrrachium.
attributes.Sources: App. B. Civ. 2.9.60: “... τοῦ φρουράρχου Μινουκίου...”
attributes.Name: Minucius

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Thelote

attributes.Description: Diogenes is described as guardian (phroura) of Peiraeus. He is later described as “over the phroura” (ἐπὶ τῆς φρουρᾶς ), when he was persuaded to give up the Peiraeus, Munychia, Salamis, and Sunium to the Athenians for 150 talents. His exact position is unclear, although it is possible that he was a phrourarchos.
attributes.Sources: Plut. Vit. Arat. 34.1-2: “... ὁ μὲν τὸν Πειραιᾶ φρουρῶν Διογένης...”
attributes.Name: Diogenes

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Theudosios

attributes.Description: Addressed as one of the diadochoi and a phrourarchos in a fragmentary letter concerning a dispute over the ownership of a jacket.
attributes.Sources: P. Diosk.12 ll.1: “Ἱέρωνι τῶν διαδόχων καὶ φρουράρχωι...”
attributes.Name: Hieron

attributes.Description: He made a dedication in Philai to the Ptolemy and Cleopatra on behalf of the epistrategos, strategos, thebarchos (governor of the Thebaid), Boethos. He describes himself as “one of the didachoi and the phrourarchos of Philai.” Besides his evident working relationship with Boethos, little more can be said about his position, other than his desire to monumentalize his devotion to the monarchs and the strategos. The second part of the inscription is almost certainly an unrelated text, and so is not discussed here.
attributes.Name: Apollonios [3]

attributes.Description: Nymphon had a long and varied career at Priene. In a first decree, dating to 277 and apparently before he held the phrourarchia, he was honored for being a benefactor of the polis. He is next mentioned in a decree from c. 266, in which the demos honors him for his actions at the expiry of his term as phrourarchos. A third inscription mentioning him as phrourarchos is dated to 262.
attributes.Sources: I. Priene 21; Hicks, JHS 4, 1883, 237-242 = I. Priene 22 = Holleaux, BCH 31, 1907, 383.
attributes.Name: Nymphon

attributes.Description: Served during the reign of Ptolemy II Philadelphus.
attributes.Name: Pandaros

attributes.Description: This Apollonios is identified as the son of Aristides and is only known from Cnidian amphora stamps found in Hermopolis Magna. It is possible that he is identical to Apollonios [1], although there is not enough information to be sure either way.
attributes.Name: Apollonios [2]

attributes.Description: According to the Suda, he was appointed as phrourarchos of Samos by Tigranes, the hyparchos of Artaxerxes II c. 370. Demosthenes, mentions that Samos was garrisoned by him.
attributes.Sources: Suda, s.v. Κυπρόθεμις: “Κυπρόθεμις: ὄνομα κύριον: ὃν κατέστησε Τιγράνης φρούραρχον Σάμου, βασιλέως ὕπαρχος.”; Shipley 1987, 136–137.
attributes.Name: Kyprothemis

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Timonos

attributes.Description: He was appointed over an unknown fortress in Asia Minor. He was overpowered by prisoners and thrown from a 600 foot cliff.
attributes.Name: Xenopeithes

attributes.Description: Sent by the Spartans as the phrourarchos of Athens at the request of the Thirty. He is also called a harmost, and controlled 700 soldiers who garrisoned the acropolis. His presence, with the Peloponnesian forces under his command, supported the regimes of the Thirty and the Ten. His position was possibly superseded by Lysander's appointment as harmost over Spartan forces in 403.
attributes.Sources: Diod. Sic. 14.4.4: “...πεμψάντων φρουρὰν καὶ τὸν ταύτης ἡγησόμενον Καλλίβιον, τὸν μὲν φρούραρχον ἐξεθεράπευσαν δώροις καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις φιλανθρώποις οἱ τριάκοντα...”
attributes.Name: Kallibios

attributes.Description: Mentioned in the dedication of an altar at Lepsia, in which he is described as phrourarchos of the island.
attributes.Sources: Sakkelion 1862, col. 265–266; Bent and Gardner 1886, 144 = SEG 18.388 = Manganaro 1963, # 21B
attributes.Name: Dionysios [3]

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Olympiodoros

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Eukration

attributes.Description: Demitrius left Diodorus as phrourarchos over Ephesus, who then sought to betray the city to Lysimachus for 50 talents. Demetrius hid himself on a vessel, and ordered Nikanor to offer to meet with Diodorus alone. Springing the trap, Demetrius sank Diodorus' boat and captured those trying to swim away.
attributes.Sources: Polyaenus, Strat. 4.7.4.1: “Δημήτριος Διοδώρῳ φρουράρχῳ τὴν Ἔφεσον παραδοὺς ἔπλευσεν ἐπὶ Καρία.”
attributes.Name: Diodorus

attributes.Description: Described as “one of the first”, a hegemon of the men, and a phrourarchos in a list of garrison members, probably in some form of a register.
attributes.Sources: BSAAlex 10 (1908) 187-195 = SB 1 (1915) 599 l.37-38: “...καὶ τῶν πρότερον|μετὰ Δρύτωνος· ἡγεμὼν|ἐπ’ ἀνδρῶν καὶ φρούραρχος·| Τιμοκλῆς Τιμοκλέους...”
attributes.Name: Timokles

attributes.Description: Only the -archos portion of his title remains.
attributes.Name: Apollonios

attributes.Description: When Mytilene was captured by the Persians in 133, Pharnabazus and Autophradates installed him as phrourarchos and Diogenes, an exile, as tyrant.
attributes.Sources: Arr. Anab. 2.1.5: “...καὶ φρούραρχον ἐπ᾽ αὐτῇ Λυκομήδην Ῥόδιον, καὶ τύραννον ἐγκατέστησαν τῇ πόλει Διογένην...”
attributes.Name: Lycomedes

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Agias

attributes.Description: The Amyzonian ekklesia decreed honors to a certain Arkanian phrourarchos, whose position was established by an unnamed king, almost certainly a Ptolemy. The stone is damaged and much of the decree after this point is missing, although some general sense can be made. There may also be a connection with guarding local mountains (ὄρος, office of ὄροφυλάκος) according to one reconstruction.
attributes.Sources: Robert and Robert 1983, #4, ll. 2–3:“ (…ἔδ[οξεν Ἀμυ]-|ζονεῦσιν· κ[υ]ρίας ἐκκλησίας γενο-|μένης)”; cf. Piejko 1985, 609 #4 who suggests this reconstruction: “...Τ[ίμων] | Ἀ[μα]δ[όκου] | Ἀκα[ρ]νὰν”
attributes.Name: Akarnian phrourarchos

attributes.Description: Phrourarchos of the acropolis in Sardis who, along with the city's leading men, surrendered to Alexander the Great.
attributes.Sources: Arr. Anab. 1.17.3: “...Μιθρήνης τε ὁ φρούραρχος τῆς ἀκροπόλεως...”
attributes.Name: Mithrenes

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Theudotos

attributes.Description:
In this inscription the phrourarchos [Me]trodoros made a dedication of an altar and flower-bed to the gods in Amaseiaon behalf ofking Pharnakos.1

1St.Pont. III 94: “ὑπὲρ βασιλέως | Φαρνάκου | [Μη]τρόδωρος | [․․․]ιου φρουραρ|[χή]σας [τὸ]ν βω|[μ]ὸν καὶ [τ]ὸν | ἀνθεῶνα | θεοῖς.”
attributes.Sources: St.Pont. III 94: “ὑπὲρ βασιλέως | Φαρνάκου | [Μη]τρόδωρος | [․․․]ιου φρουραρ|[χή]σας [τὸ]ν βω|[μ]ὸν καὶ [τ]ὸν | ἀνθεῶνα | θεοῖς.”
attributes.Name: [Me]trodoros

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Ermokrates

attributes.Description: This reconstruction names a possible phrourarchos. Dumont was unsure of the correct reading.
attributes.Name: Kleandros

attributes.Description: A restoration describes him as a member of the first philoi and hegemon and phrourarchos in Hermoupolis Magna (modern El Ashmūnein).
attributes.Sources: AbhBerlin (1937.6) 3-63 = SB 5,2 (1938) 8066, col. 2 ll. 77: “..Ἡρακλ̣είδης Ἀπολλωνίου|τῶν̣ (πρώτων) φ̣ίλων καὶ̣ ἡ(γ)ε̣(μὼν) καὶ φ(ρούραρχος)...”
attributes.Name: Herakleides

attributes.Description: He is listed as an Athenian phrourarchos by Kortenbeutel, following Beloch, There is no ancient testimony to this title; Polyaenus describes Herakleides solely as a phulax of the Athenians who was appointed by Cassander in 294.
attributes.Name: Herakleides

attributes.Description: In a letter from Herakleopolites nomos, a petitioner asked a strategos (implied but not explicitly mentioned) to request that the phrourarchos Adrastos compel an unspecified party to make a payment that was in arrears.
attributes.Sources: BGU 8.1844 ll. 21-23: “ἀξιοῦμεν ἐὰν φαίνηται |σ̣υ̣ν̣τ̣ά̣ξ̣α̣ι̣ γράψαι Ἀδράστωι φρουρά̣ρ̣[χ]ω̣ι̣ τ̣ὸ̣ν̣ | ἐνκαλουμενο καταστῆσαι ἐπὶ σέ ὅπως ἐπα|ναγκασ[θ]ῇ ἀποκαταστῆσαι...”; ll. 29-30: τοῖς γρ(αμματεῦσι) (ἔτους) γ   ̣  ̣  ̣( ) | γρ(άψατε) τῷ φρο(υράρχῳ) παραγγε̣λ̣(ῆναι) κατ̣α̣σ̣τ̣ῆ̣(σαι)”; Bauschatz 2013, 132, 212
attributes.Name: Adrastos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Ermokratippos

attributes.Description: Mithridates gained access to Heraclea through internal treachery, and installed a garrison of 4,000 with Konnakorex as phrourarchos under the pretext that they would defend the city against the Romans. He plotted with Demopheles to surrender it to Roman forces.
attributes.Name: Konnakorex

attributes.Description:
In this heavily restored and fragmentary inscription the word phroruarchos does not appear. Bias

“from the beginning continually remained in the phrourion, managing everything without blemish and justly as set down in the laws, and doing most things [in the same way], and he guarded closely the phrourion and additionally [---] of the other things that were done, those were completed without reproach...” 

The stress on his behavior according to the nomos, and the fact that he remained in the phrourion for the duration of his assignment, are strikingly parallel to other Prienian decrees. Although his official position is not spelled out in what remains in the decree, it is extremely unlikely that it deviated from the typical praise of a phrourarchos.
attributes.Sources: I Priene 23: ll. 4-10: “[— διὰ πάντα] τὸγ χρόνον ἐν ὧι τὴν ἀρχὴν αὐ|[τῶν εἶχε — διαμένων διετέ]λεσεν ἐν τῶι φρουρίωι, διοικῶν | [πάντα καθαρῶς καὶ δικαίως καθάπε]ρ οἱ νόμοι συντάσσουσιν, περὶ | [πλείστου ποιούμενος τό τε διαφ]υλάξαι τὸ φρούριον καὶ πρὸς τε|[— τῶν ἄ]λ̣λων πολιτῶν ἐν τούτοις καθ[․] | [— ἀνέγ]κλητος γενέσθαι...”
attributes.Name: Bias

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Agathokles

attributes.Description: Phrourarchos of Syene, Elephantine and Philai; gerrophylax and strategos of the nome; he dedicated part of a temple on the island of Philai.
attributes.Sources: Thèbes à Syène 320: “..Νέστω[ρ] Μελανίππ̣[ου] Φ̣ασηλίτης |τῶν ἀρχισωματοφυλάκ̣ων ὁ καθεσταμένος |ὑπ’ αὐτοῦ πρὸς τῆι φρουρ[α]ρχίαι Συήνης καὶ |Ἐλεφαντίνης καὶ Φιλῶ[ν] καὶ γερροφυλακίαι |καὶ πρὸς τῆι στρατηγί[α]ι τοῦ αὐτοῦ νομοῦ.”
attributes.Name: Nestor

attributes.Description: Roman mercenary phrourarchos who dedicated a cistern to King Ptolemy Philopater and Queen Arsinoe.
attributes.Sources: IC III iv 18: “βασιλεῖ Πτολεμαίωι Φιλοπάτορι | καὶ βασιλίσσηι Ἀρσινόηι | τὸ ὕδρευμα καὶ τὸ Νυμφαῖον | Λεύκιος Γαΐου Ῥωμαῖος φρουράρχων.”
attributes.Name: Lucius

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Timotheos [2]

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Timophonos

attributes.Description: He played a role in the local judicial apparatus. Working with the eisagoges Ammonios, he was responsible bringing accused individuals physically to trial.
attributes.Sources: P. Tor. Choach. 8 A ll. 49-41 “...καὶ μετ̣απεμψ̣ά̣[μενο]ι τοὺς | ἐγκαλουμέν̣ους διʼ Ἀ̣ν̣τιφάνου φρουράρχου| ἐπισκέψωνται”, B ll.37-39: “καὶ μετα|πεμψάμενοι τοὺς̣ [ἐ]γ̣καλουμένους | διʼ Ἀντιφάνου φρο[υρ]άρχου ἐπισκέψωνται...”
attributes.Name: Antiphanes

attributes.Description: He is known from several inscriptions from Priene, where he had a long and successful career. He held the position of grammateus for two decades (for fourteen of those years personally covering the expenses of the strategoi, the nomophulakes, and the timouchos). A second decree from Priene indicates that he later held the phrourarchia.
attributes.Name: Apellis

attributes.Description: With his fellow phrourarchos Euphranoros, he made a dedication to Athena Lindia at Plimiri near Lindos.
attributes.Sources: IG XII,1 900: “Πεισίστρατος | Εὐφράνορος | φρου[ρ]αρ̣χ̣ήσας| Ἀθάναι Λινδίαι.”
attributes.Name: Peisistratos

attributes.Description: Spartan phrourarchos of the Aeolian chora. He put on a festival, where he seized all of the attendants with his own troops and barbarians; after accepting their ransom he departed the region, handing overall command to Thibron, another Spartan. There have been some attempts to claim that Alexander held the title of harmostes, making him Thibron's official predecessor. If this is the case, then the order of Spartan harmostes in Aeolis would be Agesilaus, Euxenus, Philopedias, Alexander, then Thibron. Alexanders' ultimate fate is unknown.
attributes.Sources: Polyaenus, Strat. 6.10.1: “Ἀλέξανδρος φρούραρχος τῶν περὶ τὴν Αἰολίδα χωρίων...”
attributes.Name: Alexander

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Dionysios [2]

attributes.Description: Phrourarchos here when “Zeus was hipparchos.” The Roberts argued from the mention of the hipparchos that Daskyleion was absorbed by Cyzicus at the time, as no other city had an eponymous hipparchos.
attributes.Sources: Robert and Robert 1976, 232–235: “Πρωταγορίδ[η]ς Ἑκαταίου | φρουραρχήσα[ς ἐ]πὶ Διὸς ἱππαρ[χέω] | Ἀπόλλωνι καὶ Ἀσκληπιῶι | χαριστήριον.”
attributes.Name: Protagorides

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Philteida

attributes.Description: A Roman, he was phrourarchos in Tarentum when Cononeus betrayed the city to Hannibal.
attributes.Name: Livius

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Agestatos

attributes.Description: Mentioned in a papyrus, and was possibly involved with judicial proceedings.
attributes.Sources: Chr. Mitt. 32 = P.Grenf. 1 11 = P .Grenf. 1.11 ll. 1.10-12: “[τοῦ δὲ] Πεχύτ̣[ου]|[ γρ]άψαντός σο[ι διὰ] τ̣ῆς ὑποκειμένης |[ἐπιστο]λ̣ης κ[ατασ]τάντας [ἐν Κ]ροκοδίλων [πόλ]ει |[παρόντ]ος Δη[μητρί]ου τοῦ φρο[υράρ]χου...”, 2.10: “..συνπαρόντος Δημητρίου τοῦ φρουράρχου...”
attributes.Name: Demetrius [2]

attributes.Description: The phrouroi in Teloneia dedicated an inscription to their him. His listing is placed here as the title is strictly speaking a reconstruction, as his name.
attributes.Name: Thra[sy]boulos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Polemaios

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Epinikidas

attributes.Description: Appointed by Polyperchon to secure Arcadian Orchomenus. It was besieged by Demetrius, who crucified Strombichos and 80 other leaders after the city fell.
attributes.Sources: Diod. Sic. 20.103.4: “...Στρόμβιχον τὸν ὑπὸ Πολυπέρχοντος καθεσταμένον φρούραρχον...”
attributes.Name: Strombichos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Tauriskos

attributes.Description: With his fellow phrourarchos Peisistratos he made a dedication to Athena Lindia at Plimiri near Lindos.
attributes.Sources: IG XII,1 900: “Πεισίστρατος | Εὐφράνορος | φρου[ρ]αρ̣χ̣ήσας | Ἀθάναι Λινδίαι.”
attributes.Name: Euphranoros

attributes.Description: A Sidonian who was (restored) phrourarchos over the polis was honored by the boule of Kourion on Cyprus. However, there is some argument over the restoration and his official title.
attributes.Sources: Mitford 1971, #32: “...[ὁ δεῖνα — — — —]ς Σιδώνιος, [ὁ γενόμενος ἐπ]ὶ τῆς πόλεως [φρούραρχος]...”; cf. Bagnall 1976, 50.
attributes.Name: Sidonian phrourarchos in Kourion

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Antandros

attributes.Description: In the dedication base of a statue, Mnasis is honored and described as a member of the didachoi, a hipparchos over the men, a member of the those of the epitagma, and phrourarchos of Philae.
attributes.Name: Mnasis

attributes.Description: Ameinias son of Themistokleous either dedicated or was the honored by an inscription, found at Thebes Mykale in Ionia where he is simply listed as phorurarchesas. It is likely that the region was subject to Miletus at the time, if it was not entirely absorbed. Unfortunately, due to the brevity of the inscription nothing can be said of the powers of the office or Ameinias' role. His presence as a foreign phrourarchos does indicate that the office represented the dominance of a foreign power over the region; whether this was by invitation, conquest, or some other mechanism is unknown.
attributes.Name: Ameinias

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Boularchida

attributes.Description: A Macedonian, he made a dedication along with phrouroi from Troizen.
attributes.Name: Kyrthaios

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Sources: Dumont 1872, 132 #36: “Ἐπὶ Μενίππου | Κνι<δίον> Φρουράρ[χ]|ου.”; possibly #37: “Ἐπὶ Φρουράρχου | Μεν...”
attributes.Name: Menippos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps and is currently unpublished.
attributes.Name: Agon

attributes.Description: Demetrius is known from an inscription that he erected in a small Attalid phrourion here. His father's name (Seuthos) suggests Thracian descent. He likely served under Eumenes II, although Attalos II cannot be ruled out. The inscription names a number of men who were part of a religious association of Asklepios, which may have been entirely recruited from the phrouroi of the post. The small size of the location, the limited number of soldiers, and the visual connection of the phroura to the acropolis of Pergamum strongly suggest that this was not a settlement or significant fortress, but an observation post with limited patrol and policing powers.
attributes.Location: Ancient name unknown; near Modern Yüntdağ
attributes.Name: Demetrius [1]

attributes.Description: A fragmentary papyrus preserves only his name and office.
attributes.Name: Kratides

attributes.Description: Described as the phrourarchos with no further elaboration.
attributes.Sources: Cass. Dio 55.33.2: “... Μάνιον Ἔννιον φρούραρχον Σισκίας ...”
attributes.Name: Manius Ennius

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Name: Apollonios [1]

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps. Why Dumont has differentiated this name from Polemaios (below) is unclear.
attributes.Name: P[t?]olemaios

attributes.Description: Honored by Milesian katoikoi in Lepsia in a decree.
attributes.Sources: IIsolMil 18: “ἔδοξε Μιλησί̣ων τοῖς κατοικοῦσιν | ἐν Λ[ε]ψί̣αι·... Τιμόθεος Ἀρήτου γεν-|μενος φ̣ρούραρχος...”
attributes.Name: Timotheos [1]

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Sources: Dumont 1872, 128–129 #17: “Φρουράρχου | Ἐπικράτευς.”; #18: “Φρουράρχ[ου] | Ἐπικράτ[ευς].”; #19: “[Φ]ρουράρχου | Ἐπικ[ρ]άτε[υ]ς.”; #20: “Φ[ρ]ουράρχου | Ἐπικράτ[ε]υς.”; #21: “Φρουράρχου | Ἐπι[κρά]τευς?”. Dumont notes the significant difference in appearance in the seal of #20, although he believes there is no reason to suspect that it was produced at a different time.
attributes.Name: Epikrates

attributes.Description: Appointed as phrourarchos in Heraclea following the death of Lamachos from the plague. He is described as an enthusiastic adherent of Lamachos, and he plotted with Konnakorex to surrender the city. He personally opened the gates of the polis, admitting Cotta's forces.
attributes.Name: Demopheles

attributes.Description: He is first found as phrourarchos when he assisted Cassander by dispatching twenty ships from Athens to Lemnos.Later he mounted an unsuccessful defense of Athens against Demetrius of Phalerum.
attributes.Name: Dionysios [1]

attributes.Description: Spartan phrourarchos taken to Athens asa prisoner after Thyrea was taken by Nicias. Thucydides refers to him as archon.
attributes.Sources: Diod. Sic. 12.65.8-9: “... τοὺς δ’ ἐν αὐτῇ κατοικοῦντας Αἰγινήτας καὶ τὸν φρούραρχον Τάνταλον Σπαρτιάτην ζωγρήσας ἀπήγαγεν εἰς τὰς Ἀθήνας...”
attributes.Name: Tantalos

attributes.Description: Only known from Cnidian amphora stamps.
attributes.Sources: Dumont 1872, 131 #30: “Φρουράρχου | Κλεαν[δρί]δα”; #31: “Φρουράρχου | Κλεανδρίδα.”; possibly #32: “Φρουράρχου | Κλεανδρ[ίδα]? Or Κλεάνδρου.”
attributes.Name: Kleandrida
